extra.message: User-controlled 'filter' argument is string-formatted directly into a raw SQL expression passed to SQLAlchemy's text() function: text("title = '%s' or content = '%s'" % (filter, filter)). This allows an attacker to inject arbitrary SQL via the filter parameter on the pastes query.

User-controlled 'arg' argument from the systemDebug query is concatenated directly into a shell command via format string: helpers.run_cmd('ps {}'.format(arg)). The run_cmd function (helpers.py:9) passes this to os.popen(), allowing arbitrary command execution via shell metacharacters like ;, |, or backticks.

User-controlled 'cmd' argument from systemDiagnostics query is passed to helpers.run_cmd(cmd) when security.allowed_cmds(cmd) returns True. In easy mode, allowed_cmds always returns True (security.py:34-35), allowing execution of any arbitrary command. Even in hard mode, the allowed_cmds check has a Python string concatenation bug ('ps' 'whoami' becomes 'pswhoami') and only checks command prefixes, enabling bypass with e.g. 'echo; malicious_command'.

User-controlled scheme, host, port, and path arguments are combined into a URL and passed to helpers.run_cmd(f'curl --insecure {url}'), which executes via os.popen(). The strip_dangerous_characters function only removes ; and & in hard mode (and does nothing in easy mode), allowing command injection via backticks, pipe |, newlines, or $() substitution. Additionally, this constitutes SSRF as the server makes HTTP requests to user-specified URLs.

The ImportPaste mutation constructs a URL from user-controlled scheme, host, port, and path parameters and makes a server-side HTTP request via curl. An attacker can use this to scan internal networks, access cloud metadata endpoints (e.g., 169.254.169.254), or interact with internal services.

The save_file function constructs a file path by directly concatenating WEB_UPLOADDIR with user-supplied filename: open(WEB_UPLOADDIR + filename, 'w'). No path sanitization is performed, allowing path traversal via sequences like ../../ to write files to arbitrary locations on the filesystem. This is called from the UploadPaste mutation (views.py:186) with the user-controlled filename argument.

cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory

extra.message: The get_identity function decodes JWT tokens with verify_signature:False and verify_exp:False, meaning neither the signature nor the expiration is validated. An attacker can forge any JWT token with an arbitrary identity claim (e.g., 'admin') and it will be accepted. This is used in resolve_me (views.py:286) to set the request identity, which controls access to sensitive data like user passwords.

The Flask SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as 'dvga'. This key is used to cryptographically sign session cookies and CSRF tokens. An attacker who knows this key can forge session cookies and bypass CSRF protections.

The JWT_SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as 'dvga'. This key is used to sign JWT access and refresh tokens. An attacker who knows this key can forge valid JWT tokens with arbitrary claims, impersonating any user including admins.

The format_custom_error function includes full stack traces (traceback.format_stack), exception debug info (traceback.format_exc), and server file paths in GraphQL error responses. This exposes internal application structure, file paths, and code details to attackers, aiding further exploitation.

cwe: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information

extra.message: The simulate_load function runs a loop with time.sleep(0.1) up to 500 iterations (randomly chosen from [200,300,400,500]), blocking the server thread for up to 50 seconds. This is called from resolve_system_update (views.py:333) without any rate limiting or authentication, allowing an attacker to cause Denial of Service by repeatedly triggering this query.

The CreateUser mutation (line 98) requires no authentication or authorization. Any anonymous user can create new user accounts. Similarly, CreatePaste (line 118), EditPaste (line 140), DeletePaste (line 164), UploadPaste (line 185), and ImportPaste (line 209) mutations all lack any authentication checks, allowing unauthenticated users to create, modify, and delete data.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: Multiple sensitive query resolvers require no authentication: resolve_system_diagnostics (line 337) exposes system command execution, resolve_system_debug (line 349) runs shell commands, resolve_users (line 370) lists all user accounts, resolve_audits (line 380) exposes audit logs, and resolve_delete_all_pastes (line 385) deletes all data. These should require authentication and authorization.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The EditPaste mutation takes a paste id and modifies it without checking ownership. Any user can edit any paste by providing its ID, allowing unauthorized modification of other users' content.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The DeletePaste mutation takes a paste id and deletes it without checking ownership. Any user can delete any paste by providing its ID, allowing unauthorized deletion of other users' content.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The resolve_paste query filters only by burn=False but does not check public=True or ownership. Any user can read private (public=False) pastes by providing their ID or title. Additionally, resolve_pastes defaults to public=False (line 314), returning private pastes by default.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The resolve_password method returns the plaintext password when the identity is 'admin'. Since JWT signature verification is disabled (helpers.py:21), an attacker can forge a token with identity='admin' and retrieve all users' plaintext passwords. Passwords are also stored in plaintext in the database (models.py:14) without hashing.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: User passwords are stored as plaintext strings in the database column db.Column(db.String(60),nullable=False). No hashing is applied when creating users (create_user at line 17) or during login (views.py:234 uses direct string comparison). Setup creates admin with password 'changeme' (setup.py:63) and operator with 'password123' (setup.py:64).

cwe: CWE-916: Use of Password Hash With Insufficient Computational Effort

extra.message: The set_difficulty before_request handler reads the X-DVGA-MODE header and sets the server difficulty level. Any value other than 'Expert' sets the mode to 'easy', which disables ALL GraphQL security middleware (depth protection, cost protection, introspection protection, operation name filtering, and query denylists). An attacker can bypass all protections by sending X-DVGA-MODE: easy or any non-Expert value.

The strip_dangerous_characters function only removes semicolons and ampersands in hard mode, and does nothing in easy mode. It does not filter backticks (`), pipe (|), newlines (\n), dollar-paren substitution ($()), or other shell metacharacters, allowing command injection bypass in the ImportPaste mutation and other command execution paths.

The allowed_cmds function contains a Python string concatenation bug: the tuple ('echo', 'ps' 'whoami', 'tail') evaluates to ('echo', 'pswhoami', 'tail') due to implicit string concatenation. This means 'whoami' is never matched as an allowed command in hard mode. More critically, the check uses startswith() which only verifies the command prefix, allowing bypass with commands like 'echo; rm -rf /' or 'ps aux; cat /etc/passwd'.

The resolve_delete_all_pastes query (defined as a query rather than a mutation) deletes all pastes from the database without any authentication or authorization. This is a destructive operation that any anonymous user can trigger, causing permanent data loss.

The GraphQL endpoint is configured with batch=True, enabling array-based batch queries. Combined with missing authentication and rate limiting, an attacker can send large batch requests to exhaust server resources. The middleware protections (depth, cost, etc.) can also be bypassed by switching to easy mode via the X-DVGA-MODE header.

cwe: CWE-770: Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling

extra.message: The resolve_search query returns all pastes and all users when no keyword is provided (lines 308-311). Even with a keyword, it returns matching records without checking the public flag or ownership, exposing private pastes and user information to any anonymous requester.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: The run_cmd function uses os.popen(cmd).read() to execute shell commands. os.popen invokes a shell, making all callers vulnerable to command injection if any part of cmd is user-controlled. This is the core sink function exploited by the command injection vulnerabilities in views.py.

The application startup uses os.popen("python3 setup.py").read() to execute setup. While this specific call doesn't take user input directly, the use of os.popen is a dangerous pattern. More critically, it's called at application startup which could be manipulated if the environment is compromised.
